moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, immediately seizing control of the central e5 and c5 squares while opening lines for our dark-squared bishop and queen. This move sets the stage for a rich positional game where we'll typically aim to build a strong pawn center and develop our pieces harmoniously. The d4 opening leads to more strategic, long-term battles compared to 1.e4, often resulting in complex middlegame positions where understanding pawn structures becomes crucial. Our plan is to follow up with moves like Nf3, c4, and Nc3 to support our central control and prepare for kingside castling.

Black immediately challenges our central d4 pawn with the knight, a key idea in hypermodern openings like the King's Indian Defense. This move puts pressure on our center while developing a piece toward an active square, and it prepares to support a future ...e5 or ...d5 pawn break. Our opponent is following hypermodern principles by attacking the center from the flanks rather than occupying it directly with pawns. We should be prepared for Black to fianchetto the king's bishop on g7 and create a powerful long-range piece aimed at our center and queenside.

We're expanding our control over the center by grabbing space on the queenside and supporting a potential d5 advance later in the game. This move creates what's called the 'English Attack' setup when combined with d4, giving us excellent central control and preparing to develop our knight to c3. The c4 pawn also restricts Black's ...d5 and ...b5 ideas while opening the c-file for potential future pressure. By playing c4, we're committing to a system where we'll likely castle kingside and launch a pawn storm or piece attack against Black's king position.

Black begins the characteristic King's Indian setup by preparing to fianchetto the dark-squared bishop on g7, where it will become a powerful long-range piece. This move signals Black's intention to play for a dynamic, unbalanced position where tactical shots and pawn storms often decide the game. Our opponent is accepting a slightly passive early development in exchange for creating serious long-term attacking chances against our kingside. The g6 move also prepares ...Bg7 followed by castling kingside, after which Black will typically look for central breaks like ...e5 or ...c5 to create counterplay.

We're developing our knight to its most natural square, where it supports our d4 pawn and prepares to control the important e4 and e5 central squares. This knight placement is extremely flexible - it can later jump to e4, d5, or even retreat to e2 if needed to support an f2-f3 and e3-e4 pawn advance. The knight on c3 also supports our c4 pawn and keeps open the possibility of expanding with b2-b4 to gain space on the queenside. From c3, our knight coordinates beautifully with the rest of our pieces and maintains pressure on Black's position while keeping our options open.

Black completes the fianchetto setup by placing the dark-squared bishop on its dream square, where it exerts tremendous pressure along the long diagonal toward our queenside. This bishop becomes the cornerstone of Black's position, often supporting tactical strikes and providing crucial defense of the kingside dark squares. Our opponent now has the famous 'King's Indian bishop' that can become incredibly dangerous if we're not careful about our pawn structure and piece coordination. We need to be aware that this bishop, combined with Black's pieces, can create devastating attacks against our king, especially if Black gets in moves like ...e5 and ...f5.

We're developing our kingside knight to an excellent central square where it controls the important e5 and d4 squares while preparing to castle kingside. This knight placement supports our center beautifully and gives us the option to later advance e3-e4 to build an imposing pawn duo in the center. The knight on f3 also prevents Black from playing ...e5 immediately, since we could capture and maintain our central dominance. From f3, our knight coordinates well with our other pieces and keeps multiple plans available - we can still play systems with f2-f3 and e3-e4, or maintain the more positional approach with Be2 and O-O.

Black immediately gets the king to safety by castling kingside, which is almost always the right decision in King's Indian positions before launching any tactical operations. This move completes Black's basic setup and prepares to bring the rook into the game via f8-e8 or d8-c7-d7 maneuvers. Our opponent is now ready to start creating counterplay in the center with moves like ...e5, ...c6, or ...Re8 followed by ...e5. With the king safely tucked away, Black can begin the typical King's Indian strategy of building up pressure against our center and looking for tactical opportunities to break through.

We're choosing a more restrained, positional approach by supporting our d4 pawn and preparing to develop our light-squared bishop to e2. This move signals that we're not going for the most aggressive setup (which would involve f2-f3 and e3-e4), but rather a solid, classical development where we maintain central control without overextending. The e3 pawn also opens the diagonal for our dark-squared bishop and keeps our position flexible - we can still advance e3-e4 later if the position calls for it. This leads us into what's known as the Semi-Classical variation, where both sides develop their pieces harmoniously before the real battle begins.

Black reinforces the e5 square and prepares the central advance ...e5, which is the key liberating move in almost all King's Indian positions. This pawn move also opens the diagonal for the light-squared bishop and provides additional support for the kingside pieces. Our opponent is building a solid foundation before launching the typical King's Indian counterattack with ...e5, which will challenge our central control and open lines for Black's pieces. The d6 pawn also supports potential advances like ...c6 and ...e5, giving Black multiple ways to create tension in the center and start fighting for the initiative.

We're developing our light-squared bishop to a solid, flexible square where it supports our kingside and prepares to castle. This bishop placement keeps our options open - it can later retreat to d3 or f1 if needed, or support central advances by controlling key squares. The bishop on e2 also prepares our kingside castling while maintaining a solid, classical setup that's difficult for Black to attack directly. This move fits perfectly with our positional approach, keeping our pieces coordinated and our king's safety as the top priority while maintaining pressure on Black's position.

Black plays a key move that supports the central advance ...d5 and prepares to challenge our pawn center with typical Benoni-style counterplay. This pawn move also controls the important b5 and d5 squares while opening the possibility of ...Qc7 to increase pressure along the c-file. Our opponent is setting up a flexible pawn structure that can support both ...e5 and ...d5 breaks, giving Black multiple ways to create imbalances in the position. The c6 move is characteristic of the Benoni variation within the King's Indian, where Black aims to create a dynamic pawn structure that leads to sharp tactical battles in the middlegame.
